title: Limits to scalable evaluation at the frontier: LLM as judge won’t beat twice the data

reviews.summary: This paper studies the feasibility and limits of using LLMs (or other automated proxies) as judges to scale evaluation of frontier models, with debiasing via a small number of ground-truth labels. The authors provide a tight, black-box efficiency bound: among all unbiased estimators that only use proxy scores and a small parallel set of ground-truth labels, sample-efficiency gains are fundamentally limited by the squared correlation between proxy and ground-truth scores. Crucially, when the judge is no more accurate than the evaluated model (a defining characteristic of the evaluation frontier), no debiasing method can reduce the amount of human labels by more than a factor of two; experiments on MMLU and MT-Bench corroborate that realized gains are often smaller.
strengths: Theorem 5 shows a Cramér–Rao–based optimality of prediction-powered inference (PPI) for binary evaluations among the class of unbiased black-box estimators that do not condition on x, establishing a clean, general variance lower bound.

Theorem 6 establishes a simple and striking limit: if the judge’s agreement is at most the evaluated model’s performance (AG ≤ b), the squared correlation between proxy and ground truth is at most 1/2, capping any variance reduction at a factor of two. The extension to continuous proxies (Theorem 10) via soft agreement and calibration keeps the message robust.

The paper clarifies why high agreement does not guarantee either unbiasedness or useful efficiency gains, and introduces balanced agreement as a more meaningful quantity with tight lower/upper bounds (Theorem 12).

Empirical analyses on MMLU and MT-Bench estimate the achievable τ_max from observed correlations, consistently finding savings below 2× except when very strong judges assess substantially weaker models.
The non-binary proxy study (using LLaMA-3.1-405B token probabilities) shows practical improvements but still respects the 2× limit at the frontier, strengthening external validity of the theory.
The setup is crisp, with a consistent notation relating b, p, q, and AG, and clear mapping to common evaluation scenarios (accuracy, arena judgments, safety).
The paper anticipates common misconceptions (e.g., “high agreement suffices”) and resolves them with concrete propositions and counterexamples.

The results directly address a widely adopted but poorly understood practice (LLM-as-judge), providing a principled ceiling on promised label savings exactly where scaling evaluation is most needed (frontier models).

The work offers strong practical guidance: even “optimal” debiasing rarely beats 2× label savings at the frontier, countering over-optimistic expectations, and motivating research into settings where evaluation is genuinely easier than generation.
weaknesses: The optimality result (Theorem 5) is stated for unbiased, black-box estimators that ignore P[(s, ŝ)|x] structure; it does not rule out conditional or model-based estimators that exploit x-dependent features, richer judge outputs, or auxiliary side-information.

The frontier assumption AG ≤ b is central; while natural for binary accuracy and some settings, many practical judging tasks do endow the evaluator with extra information or tools (references, retrieval, static analysis), possibly violating this condition.

The focus on unbiasedness as a sufficiency condition for ranking (Proposition 3) leaves open whether slightly biased, low-variance estimators might achieve better ranking performance in finite samples.

The empirical section is limited to two prominent benchmarks; a broader sweep (e.g., other QA, code, safety, multimodal tasks) and sensitivity to prompt/format variations could strengthen conclusions about robustness.

For non-binary proxies, calibration is discussed theoretically (R(ŝ)) but experiments appear to use raw probabilities; the realized gains with proper recalibration or proper scoring-rule adjustments are not explored.
Multi-judge ensembles (or repeated prompts) are not empirically examined; aggregating many cheap judges could lift soft agreement above b (at some proxy cost), potentially changing the regime.

The exact regularity conditions for the Cramér–Rao–based optimality are deferred; an at-a-glance statement of assumptions (parameterization, identifiability domain, independence structure) would help readers assess applicability.
Some figures (e.g., semi-synthetic rank reversal) depend on a strict dominance assumption; more textual caveats on realism for general tasks would aid interpretation.

Classical multi-annotator modeling (Dawid–Skene, GLAD, and modern Bayesian/noise-channel approaches) and their potential to leverage repeated judgments or annotator features seem under-cited; these could, in principle, exploit structure beyond the black-box setting.

Recent lines on agent-as-a-judge, tool-augmented verification, and judge ensembles are only briefly noted; a more explicit discussion of how these might alter the AG ≤ b regime would situate the results in the evolving landscape.
reviews.questions:
1. Theorem 5’s optimality: could you explicitly list the regularity/identifiability conditions used in the CRB derivation in the main text (e.g., parameterization, independence among samples, support)? Are there practical edge cases where these fail?
2. How would your bounds change if estimators could condition on x and use richer judge signals (confidence scores, margins, rationale features) beyond a scalar ŝ—beyond the continuous score case? Is there a principled way to map such high-dimensional signals to an effective ρ²?
3. Can multi-judge aggregation (ensembles) that keeps proxy costs low push SO(R(ŝ)) above b even if each constituent judge is worse than the evaluated model? If yes, do your results suggest a proxy-cost vs. τ tradeoff where τ > 2 becomes attainable?
4. For ranking-only objectives, have you explored slightly biased but lower-variance estimators that may achieve higher ranking accuracy (e.g., optimizing Kendall’s τ with monotone transforms of ŝ) than unbiased PPI in finite samples?
5. In the non-binary experiments, were probabilities calibrated (e.g., via isotonic or Platt scaling) relative to ground truth? If not, could well-calibrated ŝ materially increase empirical τ, and might that close some of the gap to the 2× limit?
6. How sensitive are τ_max estimates to prompt formats, judge prompting styles, and evaluation protocols (e.g., few-shot vs. zero-shot judges)? Could prompt ensembles improve ρ² without violating the frontier assumption?
7. Could structure across models m be exploited (e.g., shared bias models p(m), q(m), hierarchical priors) to reduce variance compared to per-model debiasing, while preserving unbiasedness?
8. Do your conclusions change if one allows adaptive selection of gold labels (active PPI), especially to target regions where judge bias is largest?
reviews.assessment: This is a timely and well-executed paper that combines clear theory with focused experiments to derive and validate a simple, impactful message: at the evaluation frontier, LLM-as-judge plus debiasing cannot deliver more than a 2× reduction in human labels if the judge is no better than the evaluated model, and the gains in practice are often smaller. The optimality result for PPI in the binary black-box setting and the correlation-based τ bound provide a solid theoretical backbone; the extension to continuous proxies and the critique of agreement metrics strengthen external validity. While the empirical scope could be broader and the analysis does not cover conditional/model-based estimators or multi-judge ensembles in depth, the authors are careful about assumptions and discuss plausible escape routes. Overall, the paper makes a substantive, rigorous contribution that will help anchor expectations and methodologies in the fast-moving evaluation literature. I recommend acceptance.
